hero.eyebrow: AUSTIN & THE TEXAS HILL COUNTRY · WEDDINGS, COUPLES & FAMILIES
hero.headline: Made of Light\nand the People You Love
hero.subhead: We're Edwin & Joyce, a husband-and-wife team chasing the last warm hour of light so your story stays soft, golden, and unmistakably yours.
rotating: the way he looks at you
hero.rationale: I led with Angle A's headline "Made of Light and the People You Love" because it is the truest distillation of their brand: it fuses the signature golden-hour aesthetic with the human, faith-rooted ethos of honoring the people in front of the lens, stays genuinely poetic without tipping into cheesy, and sidesteps the overused "capturing moments." The line break lets "and the People You Love" land as its own emotional beat. I kept Angle A's eyebrow over the alternatives because it does the most work at once: it geolocates the recent move (Austin + Hill Country) for SEO and instant clarity AND names the three primary services (weddings, couples, families), where Angle B's eyebrow named the husband-and-wife angle but dropped the service keywords. I paired it with Angle A's subhead, the strongest of the three: it introduces Edwin & Joyce by name, establishes "we"/the couple dynamic, and names the literal craft of light-chasing that defines their look, all in one warm, premium sentence. For the rotating words I curated the best across variants rather than taking any single set whole. I kept four of Angle A's most evocative cues that breathe between literal light (golden hour, Texas big-sky light) and human feeling (the way he looks at you, the quiet in-between, your whole heart held), then swapped in Angle B's "what your kids will keep" because it adds the one note Angle A lacked, legacy and inheritance, which speaks to their family-centered ethos and the high stakes families feel. The result alternates atmosphere and emotion, cinematic yet deeply human, premium yet unmistakably theirs.

about.title: The two behind the lens
about.lead: We're Edwin and Joyce Ormeo, a husband-and-wife team who have spent nearly twenty years photographing love, light, and the people brave enough to be themselves in front of us.
body: Twenty years ago, photography was Edwin's calling and a camera was how we first learned to see the world the same way. Marriage made us partners; the work made us a team. Since then we've stood at the back of countless ceremonies, knelt in golden fields, and chased the last warm light of the day side by side. We shoot as one, which is why you'll always hear us say "we" — there are two of us reading the room, two of us anticipating the glance, the laugh, the quiet hand-squeeze you didn't know we saw.

Our family is the heart of all of it. We're raising three kids — Ethan, Lizzie, and Enzo — and they're the reason we believe so deeply in slowing down for the people and moments that matter. Faith roots us and gratitude steadies us, and both shape the way we show up: present, unhurried, and genuinely glad to be there. When Edwin isn't behind the camera, he's teaching the next generation of commercial photographers — because a craft this good is meant to be passed on.

Recently we packed up a life well-loved in Northeast Florida and planted roots in Austin, Texas. Now the Hill Country is our backyard — those open fields, big skies, and gentle evening sun-flare that make our kind of warm, natural-light work feel like home. We photograph weddings, couples and engagements, families, high school seniors, lifestyle and branding portraits, and the events worth remembering — here in Austin and wherever the road (or the light) calls us.
about.philosophy: Honor the people, honor the light, and treat every client like family.

blurbs.weddings: The day moves fast and full — we're the two of you trust to stay close, reading the room and the light so the vows, the held breath before the kiss, and the late-night sway all come home with you. After nearly twenty years and a marriage of our own, we know exactly what a wedding is worth.
blurbs.couples: Whether you're newly engaged or simply still choosing each other, we give you a reason to slow down in a Hill Country field at golden hour and just be together. What you'll see later isn't a pose — it's the way you actually are when you forget the camera is there.
blurbs.family: Kids grow, seasons turn, and the ordinary years are the ones you'll ache to revisit. We photograph your family as it truly is — a little chaotic, deeply loved — so the warmth of right now has somewhere to live.
blurbs.seniors: This chapter only happens once, right on the cusp of everything next. We make space for who your senior is becoming — confident, a little unguarded, fully themselves — in big Texas skies and soft evening sun.
blurbs.portraits: Whether it's a headshot, a personal brand, or a portrait that finally looks like you, we light it warm and keep it honest. The goal is simple: an image that carries your presence into the room before you ever walk in.
blurbs.events: From the first toast to the last lingering guest, we move through your gathering with a light touch, gathering the laughter, the speeches, and the quiet in-between. You stay present with your people; we'll hand the whole evening back to you.

process.title: Say Hello
process.text: Tell us who you are and what you're celebrating. Send a note with your date, your vision, and the people who matter, and we'll reply with availability, pricing, and a few thoughtful questions to get to know you. There are no wrong answers here, just the start of a good conversation.

process.title: The Session
process.text: We show up early, chase the golden light, and let the day breathe. As a married team, we work in tandem so nothing is missed, the two of us watching for the glance, the laugh, the quiet in-between. We'll guide you when you need it and step back when the moment is already perfect. Mostly, we want you to forget the camera is there.

process.title: The Reveal
process.text: After the session we hand-edit every frame in our warm, natural-light style, no rushed presets, no shortcuts. Your private online gallery arrives ready to relive, download in full resolution, and share with everyone you love.

process.title: Keep It Forever
process.text: Order heirloom prints, albums, and wall pieces straight from your gallery, made on archival paper meant to outlast the screen. Some moments deserve to hang on a wall and be passed down. We'll help you choose the ones that should.

faq.q: You're in Austin, but do you travel?
faq.a: We do, gladly. We're based in Austin and know the Texas Hill Country light by heart, and we're always happy to follow a story beyond it. We spent many years photographing in Northeast Florida before moving here, so destination work feels like home to us. Tell us where you're headed and we'll talk travel.

Send us a note with your date and what you have in mind. Once we know it's the right fit, a signed agreement and a retainer hold your day on our calendar, the rest is paid before your session. We only take a limited number of bookings each season so we can give every couple and family our full attention, so reaching out early is always wise.

faq.q: When will we see our photos?
faq.a: You'll receive a few favorites within a week as a first taste. Full galleries are typically ready within three to four weeks, and weddings within six to eight. We hand-edit every image ourselves, so we never rush the part that makes them yours, but we'll always keep you in the loop along the way.

Think soft, timeless, and like yourself. Earth tones, creams, and gentle textures glow beautifully in golden-hour light, and we love when families and couples coordinate rather than match. Once you've booked, we'll send a simple styling guide and we're always a text away if you're deciding between two outfits.

faq.q: Do you offer prints and albums?
faq.a: Yes, and we believe in them deeply. Every gallery includes the option to order archival prints, heirloom albums, and framed wall art crafted to last for generations. A photo you can hold is a different kind of memory than one on a screen, and we'll help you choose pieces worth passing down.

cta.headline: Let's tell your story in the light
cta.sub: Whether it's a wedding, a growing family, a senior chapter, or simply this season of your life, we'd be honored to photograph it. Reach out and let's find your date.

extras.footerLine: Made with love, in the golden hour. Edwin & Joyce Ormeo, Austin & the Texas Hill Country.
extras.statsCaption: Nearly 20 years behind the lens. Hundreds of families, couples, and milestones honored, and counting.

seo.title: Edwin & Joyce Ormeo Photography | Austin, TX
seo.description: Husband-and-wife photographers in Austin, TX. Warm, golden-hour weddings, couples, families & seniors across the Texas Hill Country. 20 years, treated like family.
seo.ogTitle: Edwin & Joyce Ormeo Photography | Austin, TX
seo.ogDescription: We're Edwin & Joyce — a married team photographing weddings, couples, families & seniors across Austin and the Texas Hill Country. Warm, golden-hour, joyfully real.

jsonld.name: Edwin & Joyce Ormeo Photography
jsonld.description: Edwin & Joyce Ormeo are a married husband-and-wife photography team with nearly two decades of professional experience, based in Austin, Texas and serving the greater Austin area and the Texas Hill Country. They photograph weddings, couples and engagements, families, high school seniors, lifestyle sessions, portraits, headshots, personal branding, and events with a warm, golden-hour, natural-light style.
jsonld.slogan: Honoring the people and the moments in front of the lens.

founder.name: Edwin Ormeo
founder.description: Photographer with nearly two decades of professional experience who also teaches commercial photography.

logo.concept: The Golden Ampersand — a single editorial ampersand as the studio's hero mark, framed by a golden-hour aperture ring
logo.wordmark: Primary: "Edwin & Joyce Ormeo" set in a high-contrast serif (Canela, Reckless, or Ogg) in a warm near-black ink. The ampersand is the hero — drawn one to two sizes larger than the names, riding the baseline like a flourish, and rendered in golden-hour brass (#B8884B / #C49A35 on dark) while the names stay in ink. Stacked lockup: "EDWIN" over an oversized centered "&" over "JOYCE," with "ORMEO PHOTOGRAPHY" beneath in fine, letter-spaced small caps (≈0.3em). Horizontal lockup sets the ringed ampersand mark to the left of a two-line name/descriptor block. The ampersand does the talking; everything else stays quiet.
logo.monogramSvg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 100 100" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round" role="img" aria-label="Edwin and Joyce Ormeo Photography monogram"><title>Edwin & Joyce Ormeo Photography</title><circle cx="50" cy="44" r="33" stroke-width="1.1"/><path d="M62.5 64.5 C53 67 41.5 64.8 38.2 56.8 C35.4 50 39.2 45 44.6 41.2 C49.6 37.7 52.4 34.8 52.4 31 C52.4 26.7 49.2 24 45.4 24 C41.4 24 38.6 26.9 38.6 30.8 C38.6 35.4 41.8 39.4 47 44.6 C52.6 50.2 58.4 55.4 65 60.4" stroke-width="3.1"/><path d="M53.8 35.5 H64.5" stroke-width="3.1"/><line x1="38.5" y1="86" x2="44.5" y2="86" stroke-width="2.4"/><circle cx="50" cy="86" r="1.15" stroke="none" fill="currentColor"/><line x1="55.5" y1="86" x2="61.5" y2="86" stroke-width="2.4"/></svg>
logo.rationale: Winner among the three. The ampersand is the most honest symbol this studio could own: Edwin AND Joyce, a married couple who shoot as one — it says "we" before a single word is read, which is the entire brand voice. It also beats the alternatives on craft and durability. The Golden Seal (proposal 3) leans on curved textPath set at 4–6px that collapses at favicon and avatar scale and renders unpredictably across email and foil/emboss; it also hard-codes "AUSTIN TEXAS," which dates a mark for a team that travels. The Shared-Spine (proposal 1) had the most poetic idea but the weakest execution — dozens of hand-tuned bracketed-serif slivers that read as noise and misregister at small sizes. This refined ampersand keeps the strongest concept and fixes the original's flaws. I redrew the calligraphic stroke so the loop crossing reads clean and editorial (high-contrast, a nod to Edwin's two decades and his commercial-photography teaching) rather than muddy, and the enclosing ring is now a true golden-hour aperture — sun and lens at once — tied directly to their warm, natural-light Texas-field signature. The serif "E · J" beneath, with a centered dot separator, grounds the abstract flourish in the literal initials so the mark stays unmistakable at thumbnail size. Rendered entirely in currentColor: it lives in ink for stationery, in brass-gold for the hero wordmark, and reverses cleanly to ivory over a photograph. Premium, minimal, scalable from a 16px favicon to an embossed album — the whole partnership in one graceful gesture.

sections.name: 1. Cinematic Full-Bleed Hero (golden-hour featured image)
sections.effect: STRUCTURE: full-viewport (100svh) frame; inside, an over-scaled image layer (img at 116% height, centered) behind a thin grain/vignette overlay and centered title lockup ('Edwin & Joyce', subhead 'Weddings & Portraits — Austin + the Texas Hill Country'). INTRO (on load, not scroll): timeline — image clip-path reveal `clip-path: inset(8% round 2px)` -> `inset(0%)` with scale 1.08 -> 1.0 over 1.4s power3.out; simultaneously a soft sun-flare radial-gradient overlay fades 0->1 then settles to 0.4 opacity; title words wrapped in spans with `clip-path: inset(0 0 110% 0)` -> `inset(0 0 -10% 0)` (mask-up reveal), stagger 0.09, y 24->0, delay 0.5; a hairline gold rule scaleX 0->1 transformOrigin left under the subhead. SCROLL behavior (scrub tied to hero ScrollTrigger start 'top top' end 'bottom top'): the image layer parallaxes y: 0 -> 12% (slower than scroll = depth), scale 1.0 -> 1.06, and a darkening overlay opacity 0 -> 0.45 so text stays legible as next section approaches; title lockup translateY 0 -> -60 and opacity 1 -> 0 (faster than scroll = it 'lifts away'); a thin scroll-cue chevron at bottom does a 6px yoyo loop and fades out after 40px of scroll. Optional: hero is a muted autoplay loop video with the golden-hour still as poster; if video, mute + playsInline + preload metadata and crossfade poster->video on canplay. Magnetic cursor active over the title. NO pin — hero just scrolls away under the next section for a clean cinematic exit.
sections.reducedMotion: No Lenis (native scroll). Image shown at 100% with a single 0.4s opacity fade-in; title words fade in together (no mask, no y, no stagger travel) at 0.3s. No parallax, no scale, no scroll-driven overlay (use a static CSS gradient scrim for legibility). If video, do not autoplay — show the golden-hour poster still. Scroll-cue rendered statically without the bounce loop. Sun-flare overlay rendered at its final static opacity.

sections.name: 2. Intro / Manifesto Text Reveal
sections.effect: STRUCTURE: generous-margin block, one large statement of ~24-40 words in the brand voice (e.g. 'We're Edwin and Joyce — a husband-and-wife team who've spent nearly twenty years learning that the best photographs aren't taken, they're noticed. We shoot in warm light, with open hearts, treating every family like our own.'). TECHNIQUE: split the statement into words (or lines) via a SplitText-style util (or manual word-span wrapping to stay license-free). ScrollTrigger trigger on the block, start 'top 80%', end 'top 35%', scrub: 0.6. Each word interpolates color from a muted sand (#C8BCA8) -> deep ink (#1A1714) as the scroll passes, giving a 'reading light moving across the line' effect; combine with per-word opacity 0.25 -> 1. Add a subtle blur(6px) -> blur(0) on the first 3 words only for a focus-pull entrance. A small eyebrow label ('Our Promise') above does a mask-up reveal (clip-path inset bottom) on enter, once (toggleActions 'play none none reverse'). Keep travel tiny — this is about typographic finesse, not movement.
sections.reducedMotion: Disable the scrubbed word-by-word color sweep. Render the full statement at final ink color immediately; apply one container-level opacity 0->1 fade (0.3s) when it enters via a non-scrubbed ScrollTrigger (toggleActions play). No blur, no per-word stagger, no color interpolation. Eyebrow label simply fades in.

sections.name: 3. Pinned Horizontal-Scroll Signature Gallery
sections.effect: STRUCTURE: a `.track` flex row of 6-8 signature golden-hour frames (mixed portrait/landscape, generous gaps), wider than viewport. PIN+SCRUB: pin the section wrapper and translate the track on X. Compute distance from real width: `const distance = track.scrollWidth - window.innerWidth;` then `gsap.to(track, { x: -distance, ease: 'none', scrollTrigger: { trigger: section, start: 'top top', end: () => '+=' + distance, pin: true, scrub: true, invalidateOnRefresh: true, anticipatePin: 1 } })`. Recompute distance in invalidateOnRefresh so it survives resize. LAYERED DEPTH while panning: each image's inner gets a counter-parallax on X (containerAnimation-driven) — give foreground frames x: -8% -> 8% and a captioned frame a slower x: -3% -> 3%, using nested ScrollTriggers with `containerAnimation: theHorizontalTween` so they scrub off the horizontal motion, not vertical scroll. ENTRANCE per frame: as each frame crosses center, scale 1.04 -> 1.0 and clip-path inset(0 6% 0 6%) -> inset(0) via containerAnimation trigger start 'left 75%'. A persistent progress indicator (thin gold line, scaleX bound to tween.progress()) sits at the bottom. Section title ('Signature Work') sits pinned at top-left, its opacity tied to overall progress so it dims as you pan. Horizontal scroll feels native because Lenis vertical delta is what drives the X tween — no wheel hijacking needed.
sections.reducedMotion: Do NOT pin and do NOT convert vertical-to-horizontal. Re-flow the track as a normal vertical stack (or a native horizontal `overflow-x: auto` strip with scroll-snap for users who want to swipe, since that's user-driven not motion-driven). Each image fades in (0.3s opacity) on enter via a simple ScrollTrigger batch — no scale, no clip-path travel, no containerAnimation parallax. Progress line hidden. This guarantees no hijacked scroll for vestibular-sensitive users.

sections.name: 4. Category Grid (Weddings / Couples & Engagements / Families / Seniors / Portraits)
sections.effect: STRUCTURE: responsive grid (3-col desktop, asymmetric / masonry-ish heights) of 5-7 category tiles, each an image with a label and a fine gold underline. ENTRANCE: ScrollTrigger.batch(tiles, { start: 'top 85%', onEnter: batch => gsap.to(batch, { y: 0, opacity: 1, clipPath: 'inset(0%)', duration: 0.9, ease: 'power3.out', stagger: { each: 0.08, from: 'start' } }) }), initial state y: 40, opacity: 0, clipPath: inset(0 0 12% 0). batch keeps stagger coherent even when several tiles enter the same frame. HOVER (desktop, motionOK only): inner scale 1 -> 1.06 (0.6s power2.out), label slides up with a mask-reveal of a small 'View →', and the gold underline scaleX 0->1 from left; a faint warm overlay lifts. Add a magnetic pull on each tile: the tile translates up to ~10px toward the cursor using gsap.quickTo(tile,'x'/'y'), snapping back on mouseleave (elastic.out). Optional micro-parallax: each tile's image y shifts ±6px tied to its own ScrollTrigger scrub for living depth.
sections.reducedMotion: Replace batch stagger with a single grid-level opacity fade-in (0.3s) on enter; tiles all appear together, no y-travel, no clip-path. Disable hover image zoom (or reduce to instant, no transition), disable magnetic pull entirely, disable per-tile parallax. Underline appears statically on hover/focus without scaleX animation. Ensure full keyboard focus styles remain (focus-visible ring) since hover affordances are removed.

sections.name: 5. About — The Duo (Edwin & Joyce)
sections.effect: STRUCTURE: two-column — left a portrait of the couple, right the story copy (married team, ~20 yrs, recently relocated to Austin from Northeast Florida, faith- and family-rooted, three kids, Edwin teaches commercial photography). IMAGE: framed portrait reveals via clip-path inset(0 0 100% 0) -> inset(0) (wipe-up) with scale 1.08->1, scrub:false (one-shot, toggleActions 'play none none none'), start 'top 78%'. Layered: a second smaller candid image offset behind, parallaxing y at a different rate (scrub) for depth. COPY: eyebrow 'The Two of Us' mask-up; heading lines stagger mask-up (clip-path bottom inset), 0.1 each; paragraphs fade+y:20->0 stagger 0.12; a signature/script wordmark draws in (if SVG: DrawSVGPlugin stroke 0->100%, else clip-path left-to-right wipe). A pull-stat or two ('~20 years', '3 kids, 1 camera bag') count or fade in. Subtle continuous: the portrait gets a very small y parallax (±5%) across the whole section scroll so it breathes against the static text.
sections.reducedMotion: Portrait and candid both render at final state with a single opacity fade (0.3s), no clip-path wipe, no scale, no offset parallax. Copy lines fade in together (no mask-up, no stagger travel). Signature wordmark shown as a static SVG/PNG (no draw-on). Any stat jumps to final value. No continuous breathing parallax.

sections.name: 6. Full-Width Parallax Image Band + Stats Counters
sections.effect: STRUCTURE: 100vw band, fixed-feel golden-hour landscape (big Texas sky), overlaid with 3-4 stat counters (e.g. 'Nearly 20 yrs behind the lens', '500+ couples', 'Austin + Hill Country', 'Travel: available'). PARALLAX: band is taller than its frame (image 130% height, container overflow hidden); image y translates -15% -> 15% across the band's scroll, ease none, scrub: true, invalidateOnRefresh. For extra cinema, give a foreground text layer a smaller y (-4% -> 4%) so text and image move at different speeds (true multi-plane parallax). A darkening gradient keeps the numbers readable. COUNTERS: each number animates with a ScrollTrigger (start 'top 75%', once: true) using gsap.to(obj,{ val: target, duration: 1.6, ease:'power2.out', snap:{val:1}, onUpdate }) and Intl.NumberFormat for the '500+' style; labels fade+rise stagger beneath. Add a faint gold separator that scaleX-grows between stats. Optional: the whole band has a slight scale 1.0->1.05 on scroll for a dolly-in feel.
sections.reducedMotion: Remove image and text parallax — band is static, image cover-fit at 100%. No dolly scale. Counters do NOT tick — set each number directly to its final formatted value (set the textContent immediately, or run the counter with duration:0). Stat labels fade in together at 0.3s, no stagger or rise. Separator drawn statically. Gradient scrim stays for contrast.

sections.name: 7. Infinite Image Marquee
sections.effect: STRUCTURE: one or two horizontal strips of small uniform-height frames, the second row scrolling the opposite direction for a woven feel. CONTINUOUS LOOP: build with gsap.to on an x-percent using a duplicated/seamless track — duplicate the image set so total width = 2x, then `gsap.to(strip, { xPercent: -50, repeat: -1, duration: 30, ease: 'none' })`; because content is doubled, the -50% wrap is seamless. SCROLL-VELOCITY COUPLING (the premium touch): read Lenis velocity and feed it into the marquee timeScale so the strip speeds up / briefly reverses with scroll, then eases back to 1 — `lenis.on('scroll', ({velocity}) => { tl.timeScale(1 + Math.min(Math.abs(velocity)/8, 4) * Math.sign(velocity)); })` with a gsap.to(tl,{timeScale: baseDir, duration:0.6}) settle via a debounced 'scroll end'. Alternatively use ScrollTrigger's getVelocity(). Pause the loop with IntersectionObserver when the strip is offscreen and on document.hidden to save battery. Slight hover: pause-on-hover (tl.pause/resume) or magnetic lift on the hovered frame. Edges masked with a left/right linear-gradient fade to black/cream.
sections.reducedMotion: Do not auto-scroll. Render the strip(s) static on a single frame (kill the repeating timeline) and, if more images than fit, allow user-driven native horizontal scroll (overflow-x:auto + scroll-snap) — no automatic infinite motion, no velocity coupling. Disable hover lift. Keep the edge-fade gradient purely as a static visual. Ensure the strip is still keyboard/scroll reachable.

sections.name: 8. Testimonial / Quote
sections.effect: STRUCTURE: a single large client quote, centered, with attribution (name + 'Wedding, Hill Country'), optional faint background image at low opacity. REVEAL: split quote into words; ScrollTrigger start 'top 70%' end 'top 40%' scrub: 0.6 — words fade opacity 0.15 -> 1 and y 12 -> 0 in a left-to-right wash (the eye 'reads' it as you scroll), plus a subtle blur 4px->0 on entry. Large decorative quotation mark scales/rotates in (scale 0.8->1, rotate -6->0) once. Attribution and a tiny gold rule reveal after the quote completes (toggleActions on a non-scrubbed nested trigger). If multiple testimonials, make a soft auto-advancing crossfade (gsap opacity + small y, 6s interval, pausable) rather than a hard slider. Background image, if present, parallaxes y gently (scrub) behind the text.
sections.reducedMotion: Quote renders fully at final state with one container opacity fade (0.3s) on enter; no per-word wash, no blur, no y-travel, no scrub. Quotation mark static (no scale/rotate). Background parallax disabled (static, low-opacity still). If multiple testimonials, do NOT auto-advance — provide prev/next buttons or stack them statically so motion is user-initiated.

sections.name: 9. Contact CTA + Footer
sections.effect: STRUCTURE: warm closing block — oversized headline ('Let's make something you'll keep forever.'), a primary CTA button (mailto:edwinormeo@gmail.com or a contact route), phone 904-305-2732, Instagram @ejophotographyog, Facebook link; footer with nav, location line ('Austin, TX + the Texas Hill Country — available to travel'), and copyright. REVEAL: headline mask-up word stagger (clip-path bottom inset, y 30->0, 0.08 each) on enter start 'top 80%'; CTA button rises and its gold fill scaleX-wipes in (transformOrigin left) after the headline. MAGNETIC CTA: button uses gsap.quickTo for x/y, pulling up to ~14px toward the cursor within a hit-area larger than the button, label given a smaller counter-parallax for a 'sticky-but-springy' premium feel; reset on leave with elastic.out(1,0.4). Contact links do an underline mask-reveal on hover and a magnetic micro-pull. Footer items fade+rise stagger as it enters. Optional: a final ambient golden-hue gradient slowly shifts (very slow scrub or CSS) to leave a warm afterglow. A 'back to top' uses lenis.scrollTo(0,{duration:1.4}). Ensure focus order and that the mailto/tel/social links are real, accessible anchors.
sections.reducedMotion: Headline and footer items fade in together (0.3s opacity), no mask-up, no stagger travel, no y. CTA button appears with no fill-wipe (static styled button) and NO magnetic pull — fixed in place, standard hover (color/underline change without animated mask). Contact-link underlines shown on hover/focus without animated reveal. Ambient gradient static. Back-to-top still works but uses native scroll (instant or browser-smooth respecting OS) since Lenis is disabled. All links remain fully keyboard-accessible with visible focus rings.
